Unmodeled Upside Potential

There is also the possibility of other unaccounted for positive developments left out of our model because they are difficult to quantify or are unlikely to happen. Here is a list of possible events that could move the needle more than our modeling shows:

1 - The upcoming SRP-1003 readout in DM1 (myotonic dystrophy type 1).

2 - Clinical Hold on the LGMD trials being lifted.

3 - SRP-1001 in Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) readout.

4 - Potential for non-ambulatory market re-entry after sirolimus/rapamycin immunosuppressive regimen for ELEVIDYS (this would be the most impactful)

Unmodeled Downside Risk

Sarepta has a revolving credit facility which is currently not meaningfully utilized. However, Sarepta needs to maintain a trailing 12-months EBITDA greater than $172 million to remain in covenant with the net leverage and interest coverage requirements - if Sarepta encounters financial duress they could find themselves in breach of this agreement.

Although the minimal amount borrowed under the revolving credit facility is not material to the solvency status of Sarepta, cross-default provisions in the 2027 outstanding convertible notes could lead to an early recall of the $1.15 billion. This poses an additional downside that exarcebates the potential solvency implications of any commercial underperformance that Sarepta may face.

As a result, Sarepta shares will be more sensitive to any earnings misses or other headwinds if they materialize going forward. For example, if the Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals programs (SRP-100 series) meet milestones sooner that expected Sarepta could be liable for significant financial obligations to Arrowhead (up to $10 billion over the lifetime of the agreement).

Financial pressure, recent investor pessimism or ELEVIDYS underperformance following the mentioned negative events could also make it difficult to fund or finance milestone payments and develoment expenses of the licensed Arrowhead assets. Sarepta could be forced to back out of the agreement, with the rights to the majority of Sarepta's pipeline possibly reverting to Arrowhead.

A first death from ALF in the ambulatory population would be especially concerning for Sarepta. Regulatory action could be swift and have severe solvency consequences considering these covenants (2027 notes, Arrowhead partnership).

Conclusion

We prefer a 20% discount rate to adequately reflect industry risk as well as Sarepta's position as the subject of recent controversy. As a result, we believe fair value here to be $30.78 per share in our BASE CASE scenario. If the situation develops further from now on (which it probably will), then this model may no longer be fitting, but assuming that ELEVIDYS remains on the market for ambulatory patients and ceteris paribus, this is going to be the fair value of the equity.

As a result, we rate SRPT a BUY, but caution that investors size positions appropriately as this company is in the midst of regulatory turmoil as previously discussed, so it stands out even in an already high-risk sector as an especially stressful asset to own.

data.title: Flexsteel Grows Albeit Decelerating, And Tariffs Are About To Impact Sales
data.description:
Flexsteel (NASDAQ:FLXS) released very good 4Q25 results (2Q25 calendar).

The name is now more expensive than after my last coverage on 3Q25, when tariff fears were high. It will be 2H25, the period that will define the actual impact of tariffs on the economy, as most companies are raising prices now, including Flexsteel.

It is back to about 10x TTM adjusted net earnings, which is not bad, albeit not particularly enticing either. I maintain a Hold rating, based on fears that recent growth was fully inventory-cycle based, and on the impact of tariffs, but nonetheless recognize the company has improved its business significantly in the short term.

4Q25 results

Revenues up but decelerating: The company posted revenues up 3.4% YoY. Growth in this context is already a big win, considering that retailers could become less aggressive in inventorying, or that the company's management said on the call that consumers are cautious.

The point to mark, however, related to my fears around cyclical versus secular or more trend-level growth, is the significant deceleration this year. 1Q25 revenues were up 10%, 2Q25 up 8.4%, and 3Q25 up 6.3%.

We should remember that Q4 is the strongest quarter seasonally for the company, after most of the furniture shows and the restocking from retailers happens. This might very well be a regular choppy quarter, especially with retailers trying not to overinventory. However, it is something to follow.

Significant margin leverage: Another positive impact for the quarter was the impressive operating margin leverage, at 350bps YoY, to 9% ($10 million). For the year, the growth was similar in margin, 300bps, to about 7% ($30 million).

Gross margins probably provided 25% to 50% of the leverage (mostly in the quarter, not so much for the year). The real leverage came from operating expenses, with SG&A actually down YoY.

This is probably one of the best points about the company today. Leveraging OpEx is key to consumer discretionary execution. It helps generate more profits during up-cycles, and to avoid the risk of being too large (generate negative leverage) during down-cycles.

This is based on adjusted figures, which eliminate both the impairment of a lease early in the year ($14 million) and the gain from the sale of a property in this quarter ($3.7 million). Both are accrual with an impact on cash. The lease impairment is an impairment of ROU, but the liability remains, only that it will not show up in future quarters as D&A. The gain from the property is accrual on the recorded value, but the cash is real ($11 million in total for the year).

Tariffs impacting pricing: The company had commented in 3Q25 that its tariff exposure was mostly related to Vietnam (55%) and Mexico (40%). Mexico ended up having a good relative positioning versus Vietnam, not to mention China.

Still, the company is receiving higher tariffs (20% on Vietnam, and potentially more on the part of the value produced in China; plus 25% on Mexico, but not for products compliant with USMCA).

During the call, the company said that it has already raised prices by between 4% and 8.5%. Management said it is at the low range of the industry, meaning there is no significant loss of competitiveness, but this will need to be studied in future quarters. Further, we will need to understand if the impact on gross margins is fully offset by price increases or not.

Valuation is fair, not attractive

The name is executing, as evidenced by growth during a challenging period, and more specifically by leveraging OpEx and therefore growing operating margins.

The company already has established a clear allocation policy of about 70% reinvestment, 30% capital returns (commented on the call), with the possibility to shift a little between periods.

However, the name is still exposed to the inventory cycle of retailers, coming from a very depressed period in 2022/23/24, which may have only started to improve when tariffs hit the industry.

That is to say, the main driver of current profitability might not be controlled by the company, but rather an external cyclical factor, against which the headwind of tariffs will hit.

For that reason, I believe that the company's current profitability should receive a discount, to account for the factor that it might be a cyclical high, and not the average.

If we stand on TTM's profitability, adjusted for non-recurring factors, it was $31 million. Without debt, the company pays no interest expense, which means all of that flows to pre-tax earnings. After 25% taxes, it becomes about $23 million in net income.

Compared to this, the company trades at a market cap of about $230 million (diluted shares 5.7 million by $40/share). This implies a multiple of about 10x, which, as expressed above is not high, but only if you believe current earnings are representative of the company's long-term profit generation capacity, which I do not think is completely the case.

For that reason, I maintain a cautious Hold. I understand, however, why other people might consider the name a Buy, as it trades at 10x earnings and continues to grow topline and leverage its margins. Still, I prefer to err on the conservative side.

data.title: SelectQuote: Efficiency Is The Name Of The Game, A Strong FY26 Ahead
data.description:
Over the past few weeks, the stock market has shown signs of nervousness at all-time highs. Investors are beginning to retreat from crowded large-cap trades like Palantir (PLTR) while breathing more life into beaten-down small and mid-cap stocks. In my view, it's an excellent time for investors to continue portfolio rotation, emphasizing "growth at a reasonable price" names that can deliver upside even if broader market multiples undergo a correction.

SelectQuote, Inc. (NYSE:SLQT) is a perfect under-the-radar buy. The health insurance marketplace geared to seniors just capped off its FY25 and introduced strong guidance for FY26, which has driven a ~40% spike in its stock post-earnings. It's a great time, in my view, for investors to reassess the bull case for this name, as SelectQuote is still down more than 30% since the start of the year.

I last wrote a Buy opinion on SelectQuote in July, when the stock was trading at similar levels in the low $2s. Since then, SelectQuote has continued to deliver increasing efficiency: operating in a tough Medicare Advantage environment with a lower agent headcount, pushing up policy sales per agent. As a result, its path to profitability has significantly widened, especially as its subscription business, SelectRx, continues to take on new members. I'm reiterating my Buy opinion here.

The first and foremost point that investors should be cognizant about is the fact that SelectQuote just released a very bullish outlook for FY26, against which the stock still remains quite modestly valued. At current share prices near $2.50, SelectQuote trades at a market cap of $419.7 million. After we net off the $31.7 million of cash and $385.1 million of debt on SelectQuote's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $773.1 million.

Meanwhile for FY26, SelectQuote has guided to $1.65-$1.75 billion in revenue, or 11% y/y growth. Against this, the company is expecting 7% y/y growth in adjusted EBITDA to $120-$150 million. The outlook does imply slight compression in adjusted EBITDA margins to 8%, but the company notes that this is driven by the company's revenue mix shift toward SelectRx, which should continue to attain better adjusted EBITDA margins as it scales toward a higher membership base.

SelectQuote trades at just 5.8x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA against the midpoint of its outlook - a rare and low valuation multiple that is difficult to find elsewhere in today's expensive stock market. Investors should also keep in mind several fundamental upside catalysts that the stock enjoys on top of its cheap price:

  • SelectRx is becoming an even more dominant and predictable part of SelectQuote's revenue base. SelectQuote used to be heavily dependent on its performance during open enrollment season, but its growing SelectRx membership base (now approaching half of overall revenue) has stabilized its business with a recurring revenue base. Over time, the company sees this segment levering up to a ~20% adjusted EBITDA margin (versus a low single digit margin today).
  • Reimbursement rates set to increase in 2026 for Medicare Advantage. The federal government recently approved a ~5% increase in reimbursement rates (flowing from federal Medicare funding into the private insurers who administer Medicare Advantage plans). This will undoubtedly improve carrier profitability, which in turn will increase appetite to advertise on SelectQuote.

Stay long here and keep riding SelectQuote's recent momentum higher.

Q4 download

Let's now go through SelectQuote's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 (June quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

SelectQuote's revenue grew 12% y/y to $345.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $334.0 million (+9% y/y) by a three-point margin.

It's important to understand that this double-digit growth, driven primarily by growth in SelectRx, is still operating in the backdrop of a challenging environment for Medicare Advantage plans, where policy features have been in flux and a number of carriers have cancelled certain plans.

In this environment, SelectQuote has knowingly made the decision to cut its sales agent force. Total approved policies for FY25 dropped only -4% y/y to 663.2k, while Senior revenue dropped -9% y/y. But the company achieved this with a substantially lower headcount. Per CFO Ryan Clement's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

As we noted earlier in the year, our agent-led model performed extremely well in a unique season. With policy features in flux and a significant number of planned cancellations by carrier, we delivered strong results during the season with an agent force that was approximately 26% smaller than in fiscal 2024 [...]

Let me detail our production and LTV metrics. For the full year, approved MA policies totaled $593,000 compared to $625,000 in fiscal 2024. The 5% decline was a strategic agent staffing choice, but we drove 24% more policies per agent compared to last year.

The agent efficiency, combined with lower marketing expense per policy were the key drivers of our margin expansion for the year. In the fourth quarter, our Senior segment produced 85,000 approved MA policies, down 20% year-over-year due to the lower agent headcount and the changes to the SEP."

Meanwhile, the company has still been chasing substantial growth in its Healthcare Services segment, where SelectRx members grew by another 2.5k to end with 108k members, up 32% y/y. Revenue in this division, meanwhile, grew 55% y/y for the year.

Adjusted EBITDA for SelectRx hit a record high at $12 million, or a ~6% margin that improved substantially from ~breakeven in the prior-year quarter. The company believes that achieving economies of scale on its agent force in this segment, plus ongoing technology and automation efforts, will be able to generate ~20% margins for this business in the long run, which is the key driver of long-term upside for SelectQuote's stock. The company notes that Healthcare Services is expected to generate at least $50 million of adjusted EBITDA in FY26, double the $25 million that it generated in FY25 and more than one-third of the company's $120-$150 million total company range for FY26.

Risks and key takeaways

With all of these upsides considered, we should also consider the risks that SelectQuote faces moving forward. The company notes that Medicare Advantage plans are still "in flux" for 2026, which could lead to another shaky sales year for its Senior division. SelectQuote's ability to predict its seasonal agent staffing needs will be key here. On top of this, we note that as SelectRx grows, its rate of new member additions per quarter may slow. The company has high hopes for substantially increasing margins in this segment from the low single digits to ~20%, which may be challenging as top-line growth decelerates.

At the same time, however, I think investors should give SelectQuote the benefit of the doubt, with the stock trading at just ~6x forward adjusted EBITDA. In my view, the company has set a conservative bar for its FY26 targets that can deliver upside surprises throughout the year. Stay long here.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Personalized Care To Sustain Revenue Growth Despite GLP-1 Hiccup
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Hims & Hers Health, Inc (NYSE: HIM), driven by its personalized care and services that have maintained its subscribers at 2.439 million as of the second quarter of 2025 from 2.366 million in the first quarter of 2025. This is a major indication of the company’s resilience in its personalized care and services, following Novo Nordisk’s decision to decline its contract with HIMS a month later, citing allegations of selling and promoting illegitimate pharmaceuticals.

As a result, the company’s stock has lost more than 12% over the last one month and a revenue missing analysts’ estimates of $552 million to $545 million in Q2’2025. Having experienced this hit, the company projects a revenue growth of an average of 44.5% or $570 million- $590 million in Q3’2025 an indication of the immediate effect of the headwind.

I believe that as personalized medicine grows, the need for physical growth is also declining, and this is made efficient by the adoption of telehealth. Personalized medicine is projected to grow at a CAGR of 17.23% reaching a market volume of $252.93 billion. As personalized medicine advances due to new technologies that enable the efficient administration of drugs, such as telehealth through licensed providers, it aligns with the growing demand for personalized care. This suggests that HIMS’ telehealth business model is an edge in offering prescribed and unprescribed personalized medicine and care to its current 2.439 million subscribers who rely on this model.

But here is what I think about GLP-1 contract termination. The loss in GLP-1 contract with Novo Nordisk was a hit for the company, resulting in a revenue of $190 million from GLP-1 in Q2’2025, which is a loss of around $40 million compared to Q1’2025. However, this is not a major reason to panic because the company still recorded revenue growth of 31% YoY, or a 7% decline between Q1’2025 revenues at $586 million to $545 million in Q2’2025. Looking at the Q3’2025 through 2025 projections, this is a reason to remain optimistic on the stock at the current dip, driven by personalized care.

Given this context, I will now explain why I believe HIMS is capitalizing on personalized care and services, which serve as the primary basis for its optimistic outlook and signal a bullish entry.

Company Brief

Hims & Hers, Inc. is a telehealth platform that connects consumers with licensed healthcare professionals in the US, the U.K., and internationally. The platform offers a range of prescription and non-prescription drugs, including items for sexual health, skin care, wellness, and hair care.

The company offers ongoing care, recurring prescriptions, and a range of over-the-counter products, including moisturizers, supplements, sunscreens, and hair treatments like monoxidil. It offers medical consultations, post consultations, and wellness products through wholesale partners. It is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

Personalized Care A Major Revenue Rebound Driver

I think HIMS is optimistic in Q3’2025 through the end of FY2025 because personalized care is expected to gain traction significantly from 2025 onwards. This area is growing at a CAGR of 8.8% between 2024 and 2033 driven primarily by the need for individuals to work on loved ones as caregivers.

For example, in the United States, where HIMS generates its revenues, 75% of US adults, especially those aged 50 years and above, prefer residing in their current residence instead of institutional care.

Currently, personal care is done through telehealth and has a market share of 57.4%. Most of the aging population and patients recovering from surgeries and chronic conditions are considering home-based primary care.

HIMS’ Position On GLP-1 Termination And Sustained Personalized Care

Given the growth projection of personalized medicine, it explains why telehealth business ventures like HIMS are experiencing an increase in subscribers. For instance, most subscribers of HIMS who access personalized medicine from its platform cite that they are unable to afford the costs of Novo Nordisk, which is selling its GLP-1. One subscriber noted that they have used GLP-1 from the HIMS platform for months without any issue at a cost of $2,000 annually, but Novo-Nordisk would make the same cost them $12,000 annually. Drawing from these subscribers’ insights, I am convinced that the Founder and the CEO of HIMS, Andrew Dudum, responded to Novo Nordisk on the company’s commitment to health and wellness for individuals as a priority. The company will continue to issue other weight loss management programs, such as Wegovy.

Here is another angle I see it from my optimistic view. The FDA concerns that led to Novo Nordisk’s termination of GLP-1 medication are dosing errors related to injectable tirzepatide and semaglutide, which patients measure incorrectly and self-administer incorrect doses. However, the CEO contends that the company has professional providers who monitor patients’ control of individual treatment decisions, which is taken seriously by the company.

Upon further examination, I find that the lawsuit against HIMS is not related to dosing errors. The FDA’s basis for the ban is overdose on injectable drugs with tirzepatide and semaglutide compounds. Therefore, it remains a marketing misunderstanding rather than a safety issue.

In my opinion, this is what drives more subscribers and positive feedback from subscribers, and is a major basis of this company’s rebound, driven by the continuous demand for personalized care.

Going by the company’s customer-focused model, which, as subscribers soar, the revenue per subscriber has now reached $73 or 38% YoY as of Q2’2025, it indicates that customers are ready to continue to do business with HIMS. Additionally, when I look at specialized care, which also includes sales of drugs to areas like dermatology, weight loss management, and hair loss treatment, these areas have a high growth rate. For example, the dermatological drugs market is projected to hit $12.28 billion by the end of 2025 from the current revenue generation of $8.32 billion. The primary revenue drivers of HIMS at 55% are dermatology and weight loss. When all other personalized services are combined, they are growing at 89% YoY, indicating a strong consumer base.

With access to Wegovy, positive feedback from its subscribers, and adherence to FDA dosage expectations as previously explained, the company has solid ground to recoup its revenue from the sale of other drugs apart from GLP-1 drugs at the moment. This is because HIMS officially ended the sale of compounds containing semaglutide offering in February 2025, and therefore, by the time Novo Nordisk terminated the contract with HIMS, HIMS was compliant with FDA guidelines. This is a marketing issue rather than a safety issue as it stands, which is why I do not see this termination affecting its personalized care further.

Financials And Valuation

In the last 5 years, HIMS revenues have been recording more than double-digit growth, with an average growth rate of about 80% annually. The personalized care and services have the primary revenue driver by over 55% and I believe this trajectory will not be affected by the terminated Novo Nordisk agreement. This is because the revenue projection for the FY2025, which is between $2.3 billion and $2.4 billion, means a 62.5% YoY. What you need to understand here is that this projection is a reiteration of the FY2024 outlook for FY2025. This means that HIMS revenues are within projected scores. This is a $7 million difference between the projected Q2’2025 revenues of $552 million and the actual $545 million, which was inevitable due to the unavailability of GLP-1 to its subscribers and this was a short term setback I think the company is adapting as I have discussed above.

Now, the company maintains its Q3’2025 revenue estimate of $570 million to $590 million. At an 82% retention rate, with 30% of subscribers preferring a more tailored treatment plan, HIMS is likely to achieve this projected revenue.

Going the same five year time frame to access profitability, HIMS saw its net losses peak in 2021 at about -$107.66 million. After this, its profitability trajectory has been on a consistent upward momentum culminating in a net profit of $126 million in 2024 and $193.6 in the TTM.

The initial losses were primarily due to aggressive growth initiatives such as customer acquisition and market expansion which saw its marketing expenses grow significantly as well as its R&D expenses. However, with it’s the strong revenue growth, the company has turned profitable in 2024 and it has sustained profitability in the TTM. Given the company’s solid growth in Monthly Online Revenue Per Average Subscriber which stands at 29.8% in Q2 2025 and its subscriptions growth standing at 30.85% as at Q2 2025, I think the company will sustain its current profitability momentum through a solid topline growth.

Additionally, since HIMS commands a market share of 39.43% after Teladoc’s 49.81%, the loss of business in GLP-1 with compounds containing semaglutide affects all competitors. Therefore, I see this as not a significant profitability hindrance. Access to other weight loss drugs still maintains its market share, which, in my opinion, is a good basis of optimism.

Let’s now move to valuation. I will value HIMS using the P/S ratio against its close competitor, Teladoc Health, Inc. (NYSE: TDOC). With its P/S being at 4.62x above TDOC’s 0.50x, it is clear that this company is trading at a premium relative to its competitor.

However, when I look at HIM’s trailing YoY revenue growth of 88.69% being massively higher than TDOC’s -2.57% growth, I think the premium valuation is justified. To estimates my price for this stock, I will begin by projecting its financial performance by for this year and next year. I am assuming a revenue growth rate of 60% for 2025 which reflects the rapid growth of subscribers in the wake of personalized care. For 2026, I am assuming a lower rate at 30% because this being a subscriber business model, I expect most of the customers to board at the early stages with the subsequent years seeing normalized growth. With the onset of profitability in 2024, I am expecting net margin to improve from 8.5% in 2024 to about 12% in 2026. Given these assumptions, I am estimating revenue of $3.07 billion by 2026 which is within range with the consensus estimate of $2.8 billion.

In my price estimation, I will assume a forward P/S target of 5x which is slightly above the current one. I think the market will appreciate the anticipated resilience of this company despite the glp-1 disruption and reward it with a higher P/S. Given my assumptions and revenue estimates, I am projecting a price target of about $68 which marks an upside potential of about 57%. As such, I think this double digit upside justifies a buy recommendation.

Investment Risks

Regulatory risks- The company is facing FDA regulations that limit the sale of tirzepatide and semaglutide injectables. This has resulted in a $40 million revenue loss from GLP-1 sales following Novo Nordisk’s termination of the contract.

Legal issue – Right now, the company faces a legal case with an allegation of misleading its investors on the nature of its agreement with Novo Nordisk. This risks HIMS’ reputation and loss of cash available in case of hearings and in case of a verdict that would warrant compensation.

Conclusion

This company has what it takes to continue soaring high. The personalized care and services have not yet been fully utilized. With a 39.44% market share, it enjoys a better reach for more subscribers and higher returns from its 82% retention rate. This results in a sustainable revenue generation basis, which is why I reiterate my bullish rating.

data.title: Qorvo's Better Than A Nice Quarter
data.description:
Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) reported significantly better than nice results beating estimates for June and September guidance. The results actually surprised us. We were expecting nice results, but nothing close to the better than nice. Even an analyst, Thomas O'Malley of Barclays, mentioned nice. Well, Thomas, you did come close. This continues our coverage of Qorvo, the last being Gauging Qorvo's Financial Stance. Before we continue, we offer our congratulations.

Management offered an interesting, rare and detailed view into the company. Thus, we included a lot more product detail than normal. And more than nicely, a new design win with Apple (AAPL) showed itself among what had been darkness now dissipating into a warm morning sun. Shall we go look! I am game. Come on!

The Company in Brief

Qorvo centers its business model around providing "the" best RF technical solutions in its marketplace including, defense, mobile products, IoTs," etc. It doesn't always succeed. A few years ago, the company developed a product for detecting health exposure, in this case, the virus. A year later, what little left-over value was sold off. Of a more recent time, management sold off a silicon carbide unit purchased somewhat recently, a business which turned out to be less than stellar. Yet, during the call, Robert A. Bruggeworth, CEO, resighted using twenty-five paragraphs a company description and commentary. Most of the writing focused on successful state-of-the-art products in its targeted markets. For investors, if long-term planning with at times several years between major revenue ramps to occur isn't your cup of tea, Qorvo isn't likely your investment. If it is, it is. We encourage interested readers to go dig in, consume and digest the detail.

The Better Than Nice Quarterly Report

Turning to the quarter, management reported:

  • Revenue of $819M vs. $775.
  • Earnings of $0.92 vs. $0.67.
  • A gross margin of 44%.
  • Apple equaling 41% of the business.
  • Guidance of:
    • Revenue at $1.025B vs. $955M and $2 per share earnings.
    • A tax rate of 15% going forward.
    • Gross margins of 49% for December.

Other highlights include ramping new parts with Apple valued at greater than 10% ASPs year over year. Also, the company reminded us that the September quarter is expected to be the highest of the year.

Qorvo is also closing and resorting its manufacturing sites which endeavor will continue into 27. Gross margins into the very high 40s and low 50s will return sooner. We suspect the driver behind this shakeup came from the position taken by Starboard.

Pull-up demand from the tariff scare seemed minimal.

Several quarters ago, the company found itself facing significant headwinds from a strategic change dropping out of the mid-range China market, a headwind that continues through this year. In-spite, the results glowed.

Qorvo's Position in the Marketplace Going Forward

As a reminder, Qorvo divides its business into three segments, ACG (mobile devices), HPA (defense and aerospace) and CSG (Wi-Fi plus). A summary of each unit and its value follow.

Beginning with the Largest Customer, parts supplied include four "categories of highly differentiated products. They are antenna tuners, high- performance filters and switches, integrated modules and envelope tracking power management." The company added an ET power management product. Going forward, Robert A. Bruggeworth, CEO, noted that the company's confidence remains strong with caveats, both commercial and technical. The technical caveats is creating laser focused execution, an area that management feels very confident about. The second, commercial, includes deals, with expiring contracts from apparently others, helps opens doors for future wins. The openings are in particular with the broadening use of the Apple modem. Envelope tracking PMIC opportunities seem possible. He also noted that the proof was in the pudding though management was much more than hopeful for increased SKUs.

With Samsung, Qorvo provides "low-band pads, mid-high-band pads and ultra-high band pads as well as mid-high secondary transmit, antenna tuning, discrete filters and Wi-Fi 7 FEMs."

Once the headwind from Android mid-range dies this fiscal year, single digit growth rates have been portrayed. For this year and with flat overall company revenue still guided, ACG revenue is expected to decline single digits. (The other two segments are expected to grow enough to create neutral revenue.)

HPA, largely defense and aerospace, growth continues with strong year over year results. It is also expected to continue for several years. In our last article, we noted,

"Philip Chesley, President of HPA, answered that the market is a $5 billion design win tunnel at this point, but the worldwide opportunity over the next five to ten years is $1 trillion, massive. Qorvo sees a path to a $1 billion annually within this business."

The defense opportunities reach into the world.

Qorvo's CSG includes Wi-Fi 7/8 with its much higher ASPs, 15%, along with Ultra-wideband, a location tool, and finally Matter. Management has invested heavily in the Ultra product. The Ultra funnel reached $2 billion of qualified SAM. In the prepared remarks,

"During the quarter, we secured multiple Wi-Fi 7 design wins across leading Android smartphone OEMs using MediaTek's Dimensity chipset. These wins are concentrated in the flagship and premium tiers, and we are engaged to support Wi-Fi 8 deployments in the upper tiers of the Android smartphones as early as the second half of calendar '26."

In the infrastructure markets, Qorvo technology leading solutions based of DOCSIS 4.0 are beginning to penetrate.

"Turning to infrastructure. There's strong momentum in DOCSIS 4.0 broadband cable access. Qorvo is a market leader, and we are well aligned with industry leaders in the continuing evolution of DOCSIS standards and capabilities. During the June quarter, we released 2 new GaN-based power doubler amplifiers, supporting the industry's transition to more intelligent and adaptive hybrid fiber coax systems."

Management summarized its market positioning with,

"Looking across our operating segments, we're expanding our content opportunity in ACG with our largest customer, while diversifying across markets, customers and product categories in HPA and CSG."

A delay in a major project slowed the CSG growth to low single digits for a year from the previous guide 10% - 12%. HPA growth rates remain on track. ACG growth continues slightly lower until the low-end business headwind ceases.

A Head Wind Summary

Some headwinds still exist. ACG's Android revenue decline 18% year-over-year, China down almost 30%. Two-thirds on the $150-$200 million decline will occur in the 2nd half of 2025. Lower CSG growth is expected temporarily from a delay in part with an automotive customer slowing a ramp by one year.

A Chart

We included a day chart from TradeStation Securities for Qorvo.

The $90 resistance line keeps showing up. On the day following earnings, the price collapsed bouncing hard off this price. Patience to buy might be in order.

Risk

Qorvo is still experiencing significant headwinds from the vision change dropping out of lower-end China RF markets. This will continue through at least the next three quarters. But the goal for the company to achieve CSG and HPA revenue greater than ACG continues. We have discussed the value of the company in former writings. The overall revenue size going forward into the next decade isn't small. The company will generated supportive margins into the low 50s with this growth. We rate the stock a buy with patience perhaps in order seeking prices in the mid-$80s. A break of $90 places the $130 range in play, strange. Notice that value shown in the chart included above. Again, our view includes a sizable market correction at some point. Purchasing Qorvo during a correction might be the best approach.

The quarter was surprisingly really nice, sorry, better than nice. Investors might be wise to take notice.

data.title: Postal Realty Trust: Unique, Undervalued And Poised For A Rebound
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Postal Realty Trust (NYSE:PSTL) is the first and only publicly traded REIT that’s focused on US Postal Service (OTCPK:USPS) leased properties. They have a diversified portfolio of properties across the US, with 1146 last-mile locations, 677 flex and 5 industrial ones, being the largest single owner of properties leased to the USPS in a very fragmented market, which in itself I believe represents an opportunity, as I mention below.

It’s also worth noting that the current occupancy rate stands at 99.8%, with a weighted average lease term of ~4 years now, which comes with significant re-leasing opportunities and driving internal growth. Although such a short-term lease would normally be seen as a risk, it’s important to remember that the USPS has a universal service obligation and they can’t choose to leave an area uncovered just because it isn’t profitable to operate there.

They also lifted their AFFO guidance for 2025 to $1.24 to $1.26 per share (~$30.33 million), achieving a great Q2 mostly thanks to the “more efficient programmatic re-leasing effort” with the USPS. This is part of their switch from flat rent leases to ones with annual rent escalators, which they expect to add as much as $0.06 to the AFFO/share in 2027. Since only 34% of the portfolio currently has annual rent escalations, that leaves room for what we can call internal growth in margins and AFFO, without the need to invest for it.

The big opportunity here is that a massive 57.1% of the current leases expire from 2025 to 2028, with a majority of them being mark-to-market leases, allowing the company to improve their future rent significantly, especially if they go for 10-year leases and annual rent escalators in this case as well. So, generally speaking, PSTL should offer great exposure to the US economy, both thanks to the vital service that their tenant offers, as well as from the fact that they should reflect the real estate market thanks to their mark-to-market leases.

And we can see some improvements especially in the AFFO/share thanks to that, with 2025 being expected to be significantly better than 2024 and the prior years. For example, based on their Q2 earnings report, they’ve added 127 properties YTD, which is part of their strategy and business model.

Of course, this has to be funded somehow, and a REIT doesn’t have that many options since they have to pay most of what they make to their shareholders. This is why we can see significant dilution since going public, although that has slowed down significantly in recent years. Still, I’ll mention again that this is not exactly the type of dilution that hurts shareholders. This is accretive, adds to the per share numbers and also offers potential to compound in the future. As of Q2 2025, they say they’ve issued over $50 million through their at-the-market and operating partnerships units since the beginning of 2024.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see an overall expectable position for a REIT, with the total assets well above the total liabilities but little to no cash compared to their debt, which is normal. There’s about $324.5 million in equity on a $477.2 million market cap, meaning a PB of 1.47, but it’s also important to keep in mind that the balance sheet is backward-looking when it comes to the value of their assets (i.e. land and buildings), being recorded at the price of acquisition instead of their current market value, which has generally gone up significantly over the years across the US.

Regarding their debt, the only major maturity comes in 2028 for $175 million, although it’s very likely to see them refinance that. If we get rate cuts in the near future, then we could expect to see even less than their 4.54% weighted average interest rate, which can be great for their AFFO.

Dividends & Buybacks

Currently, the dividend yield stands at around 6.37%, representing ~$22.8 million right now. On ~$30.33 million in AFFO expected this year, that’s an AFFO payout ratio of ~75.17%, which is not bad and PSTL definitely has room to increase it, generally being in the 75% to 80% range. I think a raise is expectable in the near future, as the company’s higher AFFO would warrant it, especially if they want to continue their increase streak since going public.

Risks & Opportunities

As a potential risk, I have to note that the company has significant leverage (debt), and as a REIT they don’t really have that many options to gain capital other than issuing stocks, debt or selling assets, as they have to give most of what they make back to their shareholders. This is normal, but the level of debt they decide to go for is what sets companies apart, as some prefer to go for a lower-risk, lower-reward type of model with significantly less debt.

However, theoretically, Postal Realty’s tenant is a solid company that shouldn’t really go bankrupt “overnight” like healthcare providers, for example, who caused significant issues in the industry. As an independent agency of the executive branch of the US federal government, even if they run on a loss, they have to provide postal services to all Americans, regardless of location, holding a legal monopoly on first-class mail and access to mailboxes (not on package delivery though, which is more competitive).

Although they aren’t funded from direct taxpayer funding and have to rely almost entirely on the sale of postage, services, etc. the company can’t file for bankruptcy in the traditional way (Chapter 11), as its existence and operations are defined by federal law, with the Congress having the final say in all major changes, including financial structure. If this company were to go bankrupt, just imagine the chaos it would cause especially in rural/other areas where you don’t see a lot of FedEx (FDX) or UPS (UPS), the company offering a vital service for millions of Americans.

Of course, with Trump and, in fact, any other politician that would want to address this company’s decades of financial distress, we even saw Postal Realty’s stock falling due to DOGE uncertainty. Depending on this tenant is never good, in my opinion, but the advantage here is that even if something happens to the USPS, these properties remain. Things wouldn’t be great since Postal Realty depends on this rent and is highly leveraged, leading to potential asset sales to offset the debt/interest, but the company could eventually find new tenants even in different industries. I don’t mean to downplay this risk, but it’s also unlikely to see the USPS gone, especially since they have a new 10-year plan to become financially self-sustaining.

To dig a bit deeper into the USPS, we have their latest report. Here, we can see a terrible financial position due to their retirement benefits. Other than that and the negative equity, the company is actually fine from a financial point of view.

What adds a lot to their net loss are these retirement benefits, but there’s a very important change here that may lead to the company improving significantly in the future. The Postal Service Reform Act of 2022, has provided significant financial relief by eliminating the pre-funding requirement for retiree health benefits. Before this change, the USPS had to pre-fund its pensions 75 years in advance (literally talking about people that aren’t even born yet). This Act forgave the billions of dollars in unpaid liabilities and allowed the USPS to switch to the more traditional "pay-as-you-go" system for retirement benefits, similar to how other government agencies and most private companies operate.

And this wasn’t a small change. We can see their total liabilities literally dropping in half once the retiree health benefits were taken out. You may have noticed that their retirement benefits went up, and that’s because in 2025, the law also requires most USPS retirees to enroll in Medicare when they become eligible at age 65.

Now, together with their plan to improve, this company could become much more financially stable, and they actually invested a lot of money ($2.9 billion in CAPEX planned for 2025) in the purchase of property and equipment. In fact, given how small Postal Realty is in comparison and how the USPS wants to improve its financials, I wouldn’t count out the possibility of a buyout, but since we’re talking about an independent federal agency, things wouldn’t be that simple. They have a debt cap of $15 billion (already reached), so until that is paid, it’s almost impossible to see a buyout, and even then, it would come with immense political uncertainty, since the USPS would basically buy back their own properties at significant premiums (has to account for current realty prices, premium on stock price, etc.), not to mention that Congress would have to approve this.

Especially since they act in a very fragmented market that has nearly 17,000 different lessors of properties leased to the USPS, with a portfolio representing ~7% of the leased market and the next top 20 largest portfolio owners combined owning ~11% of the market, there is potential for PSTL to develop significantly.

Their advantage is that they’re a publicly traded company (and the largest owner in this market), meaning they can continue to issue stock to buy these lessors (at a potential discount if PSTL is trading at high valuations) and benefit from it. As they say, this market is “ripe for consolidation” given all these facts, meaning there is significant long-term potential for them to expand, especially thanks to the rise of ecommerce and its potential to boost the value of these properties, especially with rate cuts potentially boosting the economy and increasing disposable budgets.

Valuation

For PSTL’s valuation, I’ll use a starting AFFO of $30.33 million in 2025 as they expect at midpoint, a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years to account for stable growth over time, beating inflation as they get better rent deals, a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 7.5% - above my estimate of 7.22% (assuming 9.07% cost of equity, 4.54% cost of debt, 0% tax rate since they are a REIT), for a better margin of safety. The resulting DCF valuation for Postal Realty Trust yields an enterprise value of ~$854.74 million, and after subtracting the $327.75 million in net debt, this returns an equity value of roughly $526.99 million. With 24.26 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of about $21.72 per share, which is ~43% above the current price.

Conclusions

Overall, given their financials, relative stability and opportunity from re-leasing their properties to an improving USPS, I believe Postal Realty Trust is a Buy now for a solid dividend and good upside potential. The overall risk from USPS failing remains small, while the company has the potential to expand significantly in this very fragmented market.

data.title: Kodiak Sciences: Odds May Be Narrowing For Longer Lasting Eye Disease Med Approval
data.description:
Investment Overview

In June, I covered a biotech company, Eyepoint Pharmaceuticals (EYPT) in a deep dive note for Seeking Alpha, awarding shares a "buy" rating - stock is up >30% since that note. I also referenced Kodiak Sciences (NASDAQ:KOD) - the subject of this note - as a potential contender in the same, or similar fields.

Eyepoint's lead and only clinical stage drug candidate, Duravyu - vorolanib intravitreal insert - is indicated for patients suffering from diabetic macular edema ("DME") - a serious eye condition that can result in vision loss.

Eyepoint's key advantage is its delivery system, which it hopes it will soon show in a Phase 3 study can have the same treatment effect as current standards of care, but with fewer injections per annum. Kodiak's lead candidates, tarcocimab and KSI-501, aim to do the same thing

Due to the painful and inconvenient nature of a once-every eight week injection, if Eyepoint or Kodiak is successful, its drug, or drugs, may be in store for "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues.

Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY) Vabysmo and Regeneron's (REGN) Eylea are the dominant drugs in this indication - Vabysmo revenues grew 64% in 2024, to $4.2bn, while Regeneron disclosed that Eylea revenues came to nearly $6bn, including Eylea HD revenues - Eylea HD being a newer version designed for less frequent dosing.

Vabysmo is apparently dosed every four weeks for the first four doses, and then every 8 weeks, or potentially less depending on severity of disease. Eylea HD is dosed every 4 weeks for the first three doses, followed by 8 mg via intravitreal injection once every 8 to 16 weeks

In my note I mentioned Kodiak, noting that:

Kodiak Sciences (KOD) is running its DAYBREAK Phase 3 study of its candidate Tarcocimab and KSI-501, a bi-specific anti- IL-6 and VEGF Trap molecule.

I added that:

Kodiak appears to remain firmly in the hunt for eventual approval in the same target markets as EyePoint. Kodiak's valuation has plummeted after the company reported a >900 patient study in DME failed in 2023.

Prior to that crash, Kodiak stock had tumbled from >$160 per share in January 2021, to <$10 per share in February 2022, after KSI-301 - or tarcocimab, missed endpoints in a Phase 2/3 study in the "wet" form of advanced macular degeneration ("AMD") - another degenerative eye disease.

Management put the failure down to poor trial design, and notes in its Q2 2025 quarterly report / 10Q submission that:

Tarcocimab is currently being studied in two Phase 3 clinical trials, GLOW2 in patients with diabetic retinopathy and DAYBREAK in patients with wet AMD. GLOW2 enrollment is complete, and DAYBREAK is actively enrolling patients. We intend to file a single Biologics License Application (“BLA”) in wet AMD, DR and RVO in 2026.

Tarcocimab "is designed to maintain potent and effective drug levels in ocular tissues for longer than existing available agents", just like Eyepoint's Duravyu, with the "ultimate objective of providing, once approved, a flexible 1-month through 6-month label".

Kodiak notes:

To date, tarcocimab has been studied in six pivotal clinical studies: Phase 3 GLOW1 study in diabetic retinopathy (“DR”), Phase 3 BEACON study in retinal vein occlusion (“RVO”), Phase 3 DAYLIGHT study in wet AMD, Phase 3 GLEAM and GLIMMER studies with identical study design in diabetic macular edema (“DME”), and Phase 2/3 DAZZLE study in wet AMD. Of the six registrational studies, GLOW1, BEACON and DAYLIGHT successfully met the primary endpoint.

Management says it will "announce topline data for the primary endpoint for GLOW2 in 1Q 2026 and for the primary endpoint for DAYBREAK in 3Q 2026, and should these readouts support it, file its BLA, as mentioned above.

Since I referenced Kodiak in that note on Eyepoint, Kodiak's share price has more than doubled, reaching a value of $10 per share at the time of writing. Its market cap is now >$500m, while Eyepoint's is $800m. Does the recent upside momentum hint at the fact Kodiak and tarcocimab are perhaps destined for approval, despite their prior setbacks?

Tarcocimab - Mechanism of Action & Approval Chances

Kodiak says (in a recent investor day presentation) that Tarcocimab has a mean ocular half-life in humans of 20 days, which is 3-fold longer than faricimab - Vabysmo - and that its "high immediacy and high durability" means it can become a preferred option for patients given it can extend dosing intervals from 3-4 weeks to 6-8 weeks in patients with more severe forms of AMD, from 8 weeks to >3 months in moderate patients, and from 12 weeks to >6 months in less severe patients.

For good measure Kodiak has two other therapies - KSI-501, which is an anti-IL-6, VEGF-trap bispecific antibody biopolymer conjugate, potentially a "next-generation" tarcocimab, and KSI-101, a novel, potent and high strength (100 mg/mL) bispecific protein targeting IL-6 and VEGF, and indicated for patients who have retinal fluid and inflammation.

Due to the volume of studies completed, their size, and their varying degrees of success, perhaps it's not surprising that Kodiak's valuation has struggled to recover from the Feb '22 Phase 3 miss, but with that said, it's clear the biotech remains confident of an approval for all three of its assets, with tarcocimab potentially making it to market in 2027 I'd speculate (once the FDA has accepted a BLA, it usually takes 10 months to review the application and make its approval decision).

It's also clear that, until recently, at least, the market did not share Kodiak's enthusiasm, but with several companies - besides, Eyepoint, Ocular Therapeutix (OCUL) is another - now close to sharing Phase 3 data for less frequently dosed alternatives to Eylea / Vabysmo, it is beginning to feel as though at least one drug may show non-inferiority to the incumbents, and make it to market, with an obvious competitive advantage, opening up a significant market opportunity.

It is therefore tempting to wonder if Kodiak's past study failures were genuinely down to poor study design and if the Palo Alto, California based company can restore its reputation - and its share price - with a resounding study win that sets up an approval?

It's intriguing that Kodiak seems to be evaluating two drugs in the same pivotal study - I am not sure I have come across this before - but it does theoretically improve the chances of a study success. If the old stager tarcocimab demonstrates once again that it cannot match the efficacy of aflibercept - Eylea - then perhaps the next-generation version, KSI-501, can?

If tarcocimab does not succeed in Daybreak, it has a shot of success in diabetic retinopathy ("DR"), given the GLOW-2 study has a similar study design to GLOW-1, a study in which it met its primary endpoint of proportion of patients with ≥2-Step improvement in DRSS (diabetic retinopathy severity score) from Baseline to Week 48.

Meanwhile, in its BEACON study in RVO, the drug was able to match efficacy with aflibercept, while 47% of patients treated with four initiating doses of tarcocimab did not require additional injections in the second half the year, compared to 37% of patients treated with six initial injections of Eylea.

KSI-501 has undergone fewer studies than tarcocimab, so less is known about its safety efficacy profile, although it is designed to act in a similar fashion to tarcocimab, as shown below:

Here is where Kodiak believes its two therapies may one day sit within the eye disease "Golden Quadrant"

Analysis - Is Kodiak Worth Backing As A Breakout Star In A >$15bn Dollar Market?

Tarcocimab failed its Phase 2/3 in Wet AMD because - management believes - after the initial loading doses, its drug was administered no more than once every 3 months, and sometimes less frequently, while afibercept patients were treated every two months. In short, Kodiak sets itself too difficult a challenge, although management also noted:

A pre-specified secondary analysis at year 1 assessing durability showed 59% of patients in the KSI-301 arm achieved five-month dosing with visual acuity gains and anatomic improvements comparable to the overall aflibercept group.

Tarcocimab did not succeed in its GLEAM and GLIMMER studies in DME due to a "higher-than-expected increase in cataracts seen in the tarcocimab arms of both trials", however DME is no longer a target indication for the drug.

My interpretation of Kodiak's results - successful as well as failed - is that they have all been relatively close run, which shows that tarcocimab has some merit, and would not be a dangerous or an ineffective drug if it were to be approved, but equally, may not be as significantly differentiated from Eylea HD and Vabysmo in terms of dosing frequency as Kodiak management and shareholders may hope.

Nevertheless, the rise in sales of Vabysmo compared to original Eylea seems to highlight the fact that less frequent dosing regimes are popular with patients, who would be prepared to switch to whichever therapy is administered least frequently, without sacrificing efficacy.

Kodiak reported $104.2m of cash (as of the end of Q2 2025), sufficient to last into 2026, management says. - net loss in Q2 was $(54.3m).

There are some major data catalysts upcoming early next year for Kodiak, and because of the company's limited funding, if they are not positive (and especially so for tarcocimab, given it could potentially be approved without any further studies required) I think that may put Kodiak's future as a listed company in doubt, because it will be a third major study failure for the candidate, and it will be very hard for the company to raise funding - to continue studies of other assets - at a competitive price.

If Tarcocimab succeeds, I can see the share price spiking upward, but the fact that it would be another 18 months perhaps, and hundreds of millions dollars spent, to secure approval for the drug, by which time it will enter a market crammed with cheap generic versions of Eylea (whose patents are expired), I don't find myself sold on the prospect of sustained growth in the share price.

Unfortunately, this analysis of Kodiak comes a little too late, given shares have enjoyed their best bull run in years in recent weeks, and at a market cap of $500m, and given the risks I have discussed above, I don't find the risk / reward analysis compelling enough to support a Buy call.

Nevertheless, if there is any downside share price drift - as is quite common for biotechs as the markets waits impatiently for data - then I would consider a speculative purchase of the stock, not least because, if tarcocimab or KSI-501 show best-in-class efficacy, I would not rule out the possibility of Regeneron moving to acquire the company, for sum likely significantly >$1bn, I'd speculate.

data.title: PEJ: A Stealthy Play On Fed Rate Cuts
data.description:
Markets had been girding for a potential resumption of the Fed’s rate-cutting cycle. The sellside was hard at work sussing out the signal from noise, should a quarter-point ease be on the way on September 17.

According to both Bank of America Global Research and JP Morgan, the Consumer Discretionary sector tends to do well around a Fed rate cut after a pause. One of the best ways you can play is, of course, to buy a standard index ETF tracking discretionary.

But let’s get more granular. With ongoing consumer spending and the prioritization of experiences over things, I assert that owning the Invesco Dynamic Leisure and Entertainment ETF (NYSEARCA:PEJ) could work looking ahead a handful of quarters. I have a buy rating on the fund.

Consumer Discretionary Strength Outside of Fed Tightening

Discretionary Often Does Well After the Fed Resumes Cutting

According to the issuer, PEJ owns shares of companies based on a variety of investment merit criteria, including price momentum, earnings momentum, quality, management action, and value. The Index comprises common stocks of 30 US leisure and entertainment companies. These are companies that are principally engaged in the design, production, or distribution of goods or services in the leisure and entertainment industries.

PEJ is a small ETF with just $354 million in assets under management as of August 21, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 57 basis points, about a half of a percentage point above the cheapest Consumer Discretionary index ETFs, while there is no trailing 12-month dividend yield.

Share-price momentum has been solid in the last four-plus months, earning the product a robust B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Still, PEJ has been risky this year, with its high exposure to a very cyclical niche of the US economy.

From a liquidity perspective, its average daily volume is low, just 33,000 shares, and the 30-day median bid/ask spread is a bit wide at 10 basis points. Thus, I assert that prospective investors should use limit orders, particularly around the market’s open.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 2-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box, which is a good thing from a diversification point of view. 97% of the allocation is US, so there’s hardly any geographic mix. But with a price-to-earnings ratio of less than 17x, PEJ trades at a significant discount to the S&P 500. The long-term EPS growth rate is likewise respectable, at 9.0%.

PEJ: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As far as the sector breakdown goes, Consumer Discretionary is by far the biggest weight at 57%. Communication Services is technically an overweight compared to its position in the S&P 500, but holdings there are also broadly tied to the US consumer.

With significant exposure to areas like cruise lines and airlines, investors should expect volatility with PEJ.

PEJ: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal point of view, we are in the heart of a doldrums period. The August through October stretch has historically produced tepid returns. November, however, has been far and away the best month on the calendar when scanning price return data over the past 10 years.

PEG: Neutral Near-Term Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a fair valuation and offering potential alpha if the usual pattern of consumer equity strength unfolds in the event of a Fed rate cut, PEJ’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, asserting that the bulls control the primary trend. What’s more, shares tagged a new all-time high in July. That move, however, came on a modestly weaker RSI momentum oscillator reading.

Then, just this month, the ETF made a run at another new high, but it has failed so far, while the RSI has weakened significantly. I’d like to see a solid upward thrust on volume. If that doesn’t play out, support should come into play around the $55 mark. There is a small gap there just below the August low. Below that, long-term buyers should step up close to $50—where another gap lingers and where the fund bottomed in January.

PEJ: Momentum Easing With Shares Near the High

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on PEJ. The travel & leisure portfolio has consolidated so far in the second half after a stellar rebound off the post-Liberation Day low. If we do see Fed rate cuts come to fruition, this might be a stealthy outperformer.

data.title: IVE: The Growth ETF Marketing Itself To Be A Value One
data.description:
Growth stocks have been dominating the market in the last few years, resulting in global indices being driven by only a few stocks and record high valuation metrics.

In this environment, I recommend a buy on value companies, but not on the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (NYSEARCA:IVE), which I rate a hold. A quick look at this fund’s portfolio and valuation metrics reveals some inconsistencies.

The Case for Value Investing

The value investing style, with key figures such as Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett is aimed at companies trading below their intrinsic value. The exact way to identify a value stock is not fixed, but valuation metrics like the P/E and P/B ratios are a great start. Also, a balanced approach of earnings, book value, and dividends provides an intuitive and common sense way of screening for value. I believe that any well balanced portfolio should contain a value component, useful for risk to lower risks and improve diversification.

Historically, it is clear that there are periods favoring value stocks, and others favoring growth stocks, and it's not easy to predict what the next few years will be like. Since late 2020, value has outperformed growth, but looking back between 2007 and 2020, the value style was largely beaten by growth companies, JP Morgan calling this period “a lost decade”. The explanation behind that is that between 2007 and 2020, we were in a period of secular stagnation and growth was hard to find: everyone was buying the same companies, and multiples were exploding. Additionally, the low growth low inflation environment favored low interest rates, supporting growth companies expansion with virtually free money. Since 2020, the economic environment has become tense, favoring back value companies. However, they still have not recovered the gap since 2007.

With a very long term scope, looking back to 1927, value stocks have delivered a 4.4% outperformance against growth, even taking into account the recent underperformance. If you look at the graph below and the years when the value style grew the most, it was when you had tense economic conditions and high interest rates, supporting companies with stable cash flows and dividends. Seems familiar ? I think this is close to what we are seeing now.

The S&P 500, now driven by growth companies with 7 of them accounting for most of the performance, as their weight in the index is growing relentlessly (see below):

Mechanically, this means that the index P/E ratio is exploding, reaching similar heights than during the dotcom bubble.

When I see this, I have a hard time justifying buying S&P 500 focused index, even if I still think some growth industries are interesting (such as semiconductors). Any investor looking at this chart should ask if we are or not in a bubble, and if they should increase or not the value component of their portfolio.

Some quick facts are also pointing in the direction of owning value companies:

  • Inflation metrics have never been more volatile in the last 3 decades.

  • Assets have never been so reactive to the macroeconomic environment:

  • New forecasts are indicating that interest rates won’t return to pre-pandemic levels in developed countries, when in 2023 it was generally admitted that it would be the case.

  • Global growth is slowing (I said earlier that this can benefit growth companies' valuations, but it was true in a low rate environment).

  • The share of value companies in the S&P 500 index is declining:

  • Value companies are 40% below their historical long term valuation levels and valuations of value versus growth are very near all-time lows:

Sectoral Strengths and Peer Insights

IVE is tracking the S&P 500 Value Index. This index selects value companies based on valuation metrics (ratios of book value, earnings and sales to price). The fund holds 398 companies with a top 10 concentration of 28.49%. Most of the top 10 concentration is coming from the top 3: Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN). This is a really debatable choice and when I make the case for value companies, these are not the first companies I think about.

Other portfolio companies such as Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) make sense.

Looking at the sectoral split, the fund has a large allocation to IT companies (almost 25%), again this is not how I pictured the top sector of a value fund. It is followed by financials and health care for 30% of the fund and then the rest is diversified across consumer discretionary, industrials, consumer staples, energy and utilities. You will indeed find value sectors in that split, but I don’t like the repartition, which is too growth-oriented to be the fund that you buy to increase the share of value companies that you own in your portfolio.

Another value ETF I identified is the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV), which is tracking the CRSP US Large Cap Value Index. This ETF holds 323 companies with a 20.85% top 10 concentration. The top 3 account for 9.06% of the portfolio (more diversified than IVE) and is represented by Berkshire Hathaway, JPMorgan (JPM) and Exxon. Just with the top 3, I see more of a value portfolio than I did with IVE. Looking at the sectoral split, the top 3 allocations are financials, industrials and health care for close to 50% of the portfolio. This is what is called a value portfolio.

You understand it by now, I have nothing against IVE, but I don’t think it is a value ETF, and it is clear when we compare it with a “real” one.

Financial Metrics

The fund’s expense ratio is expensive, 0.18% for such a fund is high, for example VTV’s expense ratio is 0.04%, more than 4x cheaper. The fund manages $40.7 Bn and has a daily trading volume of $87 Mn. Reflecting on positioning of VTV that makes more sense, the investor appetite also is stronger, the fund manages almost $200 Bn.

The fund distributes a 1.86% dividend yield backed by a 1.80% 30-day SEC yield but is outshined by VTV at 2.14%.

Looking at valuation metrics will confirm my initial thoughts on the “value” of IVE: the fund’s P/E ratio is 22.78. VTV’s P/E ratio is 19.6 P/E.

Now I will look at both funds’ performance below:

Despite its more aggressive positioning, IVE is not even beating VTV. Of course the fund still delivers decent returns, and this is why I will rate it as a hold.

Risks

  • Concentration Risk:

The top 2 assets both have a weight above 7%, which is high (relative to the rest of the portfolio) and introduces company specific risk, especially given since we are talking about tech companies. Holding tech giants exposes the fund to regulatory scrutiny like antitrust actions.

The fund is concentrated on IT companies, almost 25% of the fund. This is not a value sector (even if I don’t deny you can find value IT companies) and expose the fund to the volatility of a sector with high valuations.

  • Liquidity Risk:

The daily trading volume of the fund is $87 Mn, which might seem high. However, it is only 0.2% of the fund’s AUM. I find it to be small and it could imply wide bid-ask spreads on high volume days.

  • Macroeconomic Uncertainty:

I would say that value companies are less exposed to macroeconomic uncertainty (inflation volatility, the target of interest rates and the cutting pace, the tariff environment, the slowing global GDP, etc…) but IVE is growth-oriented for a value ETF and this means it is more exposed to these factors than what you would initially expect when buying value companies.

Conclusion

The iShares S&P 500 Value ETF is not as much of a value ETF as you would initially expect. I am not saying it is a bad fund, but I think it is more aimed at replacing your S&P 500 ETF than complementing it to increase your share of value companies. In other words, I think the fund’s name and marketing is misleading. For example, in the fund’s website, one of the reasons why you should buy IVE is “Use as a complement to a portfolio's core holdings”. I don’t think it is what the fund is doing.

If you are looking for a value-focused ETF, I don’t recommend buying IVE.
